name: Antigua Guatemala
summary: Antigua Guatemala is one of Guatemala's safest tourist destinations, with a strong tourist police presence and well-developed infrastructure for visitors. While petty crime exists, violent crime against tourists is relatively rare, and the city's colonial center is generally safe to explore.
quickVerdict: Antigua Guatemala stands out as a relatively safe destination within Guatemala, benefiting from dedicated tourist police (DISETUR), the ASISTUR program, and its status as a UNESCO World Heritage site. While visitors should remain cautious about petty theft and avoid certain areas at night, the city's main tourist zones are well-patrolled and most travelers report feeling secure. The biggest risks are pickpocketing, ATM fraud, and natural hazards from nearby volcanoes.

neighborhoods.name: Historic Center/Central Plaza
neighborhoods.description: The heart of Antigua with heavy tourist police presence, well-lit streets, and constant activity. Safest area for walking and exploring during both day and night.

neighborhoods.name: La Merced Area
neighborhoods.description: Popular tourist zone with restaurants, hostels, and shops. Good lighting and regular police patrols make it relatively secure.

neighborhoods.name: Cerro de la Cruz
neighborhoods.description: Scenic viewpoint that's safe during daylight hours with groups, but isolated location makes solo visits risky. Tourist police sometimes provide escorts.

neighborhoods.name: Cemetery Area
neighborhoods.description: Less touristy area that can feel unsafe, especially after dark. Avoid walking alone and consider taking tuk-tuks in this area.

neighborhoods.name: Zona 3 (Outskirts)
neighborhoods.description: Local residential area with higher crime rates. Tourists should avoid this zone, especially at night, as it lacks tourist infrastructure and police presence.

neighborhoods.name: Market Area (Late Night)
neighborhoods.description: Busy during day but becomes isolated after vendors close. Higher risk of petty crime in evening hours when foot traffic decreases.

scams.name: ATM Skimming
scams.description: Criminals place devices on ATMs to steal card information, particularly around Central Plaza and popular tourist areas.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs inside banks, cover your PIN, check for unusual devices, and monitor your accounts regularly.

scams.name: Fake Police Checkpoints
scams.description: Criminals pose as police to extort money or steal documents, especially on roads leading to/from Antigua.
scams.howToAvoid: Ask for proper identification, carry only photocopies of documents, and report suspicious encounters to real police.

scams.name: White Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Unlicensed white taxis often overcharge tourists and may be involved in criminal activities.
scams.howToAvoid: Use yellow taxis, Uber, or hotel-recommended transport. Agree on fares beforehand for any taxi without a meter.

scams.name: Distraction Theft
scams.description: Teams work together - one person spills something on you while an accomplice steals your belongings.
scams.howToAvoid: Stay alert in crowded areas, keep bags secure, and be suspicious of unexpected 'accidents' or overly helpful strangers.

soloFemale.overview: Antigua is generally considered safe for solo female travelers, with many women reporting positive experiences. The city has good tourist infrastructure and police presence, though standard precautions are necessary. Female travelers should be aware of occasional catcalling and avoid walking alone after dark.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed hostels in safe neighborhoods like the Historic Center
Avoid walking alone after 9-10 PM, especially to isolated areas like Cerro de la Cruz
Use Uber or tuk-tuks for nighttime transportation rather than walking
Join group tours for volcano hikes and day trips rather than going solo
Trust your instincts and remove yourself from uncomfortable situations

nightSafety.overview: Antigua's nightlife scene is relatively safe in the main tourist areas, with bars and clubs staying open until around 1 AM. The Historic Center remains fairly well-patrolled at night, but caution is advised when moving between venues or returning to accommodations.
tips: Stay in groups when bar hopping and avoid leaving drinks unattended
Stick to well-lit main streets and avoid shortcuts through dark alleys
Use tuk-tuks or arrange transport rather than walking long distances at night
Be back before bars close at 1 AM as streets become emptier after
Avoid the cemetery area and Zone 3 completely after dark

transport.metro: Antigua has no metro system as it's a small colonial city.
transport.rideshare: Uber operates reliably in Antigua and is considered the safest transport option. InDriver is available but less secure.
transport.taxis: Yellow taxis are metered and safer than white taxis. White taxis are unregulated and often overcharge tourists. Hotel-arranged taxis are generally trustworthy.
transport.tips: Use Uber when possible, avoid white taxis, use tourist shuttles between cities rather than chicken buses, and don't drive at night outside the city.

customs: Greet people with 'Buenos días/tardes/noches' when entering shops or restaurants
Ask permission before photographing people, especially indigenous individuals
Dress modestly when visiting churches or traditional communities - cover shoulders and knees
Remove shoes when entering someone's home
Use formal address (usted) rather than informal (tú) when speaking Spanish

health.overview: Antigua has better healthcare than most of Guatemala, with private clinics and English-speaking doctors available. However, medical facilities are still below US/European standards.
health.water: Stick to bottled water and avoid ice. Many restaurants serve safe filtered water but confirm first.
health.vaccinations: Routine vaccines plus Hepatitis A/B recommended. Malaria risk is low in Antigua due to altitude. Consult a travel clinic before departure.
health.altitude: Antigua sits at 5,000 feet - some visitors may experience mild altitude effects initially. Climate is spring-like year-round with rainy season May-October.

emergency.usEmbassy: U.S. Embassy Guatemala City: Boulevard Austriaco 11-51, Zone 16, Guatemala City. Emergency: +502-2326-4000. After hours: +1-202-501-4444

faq.q: Is Antigua Guatemala safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, Antigua is one of Guatemala's safest cities for tourists with dedicated tourist police, good infrastructure, and relatively low violent crime rates. However, visitors should still take standard precautions against petty theft.

faq.q: Is Antigua Guatemala safe at night?
faq.a: The Historic Center is generally safe at night with good lighting and police presence, but avoid walking alone after 10 PM. Use tuk-tuks or Uber for transportation, and stick to main tourist areas.

faq.q: Is Antigua Guatemala safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Many solo female travelers visit Antigua safely. Stay in well-reviewed accommodations, avoid isolated areas at night, use group tours for activities, and trust your instincts about situations that feel uncomfortable.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Antigua Guatemala?
faq.a: Avoid Zona 3 (outskirts), the cemetery area at night, and isolated paths around Cerro de la Cruz when alone. Don't venture into local residential neighborhoods outside the tourist zone.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Antigua Guatemala?
faq.a: Uber is the safest option. Yellow taxis are acceptable but avoid white taxis. For longer distances, use tourist shuttles rather than local 'chicken buses' which have higher crime rates.
